en.extract:
The Socorro wren (Troglodytes sissonii) is a species of bird in the family Troglodytidae.
It is endemic to Socorro Island, Mexico. It was formerly placed in Thryomanes but was moved to Troglodytes considering "manners, song, plumage, etc." and by biogeography and mitochondrial DNA sequence analysis of the MT-NADH dehydrogenase protein 2 gene.
Its natural habitats is the tropical arid Croton masonii–prickly pear shrubland and occasionally the more humid forest. It appears to prefer this habitat, limiting its presence above 600 m (2,000 ft) although it does range over the whole island.
During visits in 1953, the birds were observed to be out of breeding season in mid-March; territorial males were observed in mid-late November.
The main limiting factors are by habitat destruction due to feral sheep and predation by feral cats. In 1953, it was noted to be unwary. However, the IUCN does not yet consider it threatened, because especially compared to the larger birds of Socorro, it has fared not too badly and remains among the most common local landbirds.

The '''Socorro wren''' ('''''Troglodytes sissonii''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Troglodytidae]].
It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Socorro Island]], [[Mexico]]. It was formerly placed in ''[[Thryomanes]]'' but was moved to ''Troglodytes'' considering "manners, song, plumage, etc."<ref>{{cite book|author=Howell, Steven N.G. |author2= Webb, Sophie |year=1995|title=A Guide to the Birds of Mexico and Northern Central America |publisher=Oxford University Press|location= Oxford & New York|isbn=0-19-854012-4}}</ref> and by [[biogeography]] and [[mitochondrial DNA]] [[sequence analysis]] of the [[MT-ND2|MT-NADH dehydrogenase protein 2 gene]].<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Martínez Gómez |first1=Juan E. |last2=Barber |first2=Brian R. |last3=Peterson |first3=A. Townsend |title=Phylogenetic position and generic placement of the Socorro Wren (''Thryomanes sissonii'')|doi=10.1642/0004-8038(2005)122[0050:PPAGPO]2.0.CO;2|url=https://academic.oup.com/auk/article/122/1/50/5562400|year=2005|volume=122|issue=1|pages=50–56|journal=[[Ornithology_(journal)|The Auk]]|hdl=1808/16612|s2cid=20879561 |hdl-access=free}}</ref>

Its natural [[habitat]]s is the tropical [[arid]] ''[[Croton masonii]]''–[[Opuntia|prickly pear]]{{#tag:ref|Probably [[Engelmann's Prickly Pear]] (''Opuntia engelmannii'').<ref name=b&h1956/>|group=Note}} [[shrubland]] and occasionally the more [[humid]] [[forest]]. It appears to prefer this habitat, limiting its presence above {{cvt|600|m|ft|-2}} although it does range over the whole island.<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=b&h1956/>

During visits in 1953, the birds were observed to be out of breeding season in mid-March; territorial males were observed in mid-late November.<ref name = b&h1956>{{cite journal|author1=Brattstrom, Bayard H.  |author2=Howell, Thomas R.  |name-list-style=amp |year=1956|title= The Birds of the Revilla Gigedo Islands, Mexico|journal=[[The Condor (journal)|The Condor]]|volume=58|issue=2|pages= 107–120|doi=10.2307/1364977|jstor=1364977 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v058n02/p0107-p0120.pdf}}</ref>

The main limiting factors are by [[habitat destruction]] due to [[feral]] sheep and predation by feral cats. In 1953, it was noted to be unwary.<ref name = b&h1956 /> However, the [[IUCN]] does not yet consider it threatened, because especially compared to the larger birds of Socorro, it has fared not too badly and remains among the most common local landbirds.<ref name=IUCN/>
